summary.intent: Implement all 9 findings from a Codex pass-2 review of the `tags_input` component in the ars-ui Rust library, including a user-confirmed ARIA anatomy change (TagDeleteCell wrapper part) and RTL keyboard navigation support.
summary.outcome: All 9 fixes were applied, tests grew from 135 to 145 passing, snapshots updated and accepted, spec synced, clippy clean, coverage gate passed (90.1% branch / 97.56% line), and changes committed and pushed to the impl-242 worktree.

repo: The component layer enforces a strict three-way sync: source (mod.rs), spec conformance tests (tests/spec_conformance/selection.rs), and the markdown spec (spec/components/selection/tags-input.md) must all be updated together or CI fails.

Parts are dispatched through a `ConnectApi` match arm; adding a new Part (TagDeleteCell) requires updates to the Part enum, the attr method, the ConnectApi dispatcher, the conformance anatomy list, and the `part_attrs_dispatch_covers_every_part` test.

Snapshot tests cover rendered attr sets; intentional attr changes require explicit snapshot acceptance (`cargo test -- --update`) and must be reviewed before accepting to confirm they match only the intended diff.

The `xci-fast` gate runs 10 sub-gates (build, tests, spec conformance, snapshots, clippy, proptest, coverage branch ≥90%, coverage line, spec validator, snippet compiler); all must be green before push.
Direction::Auto is a valid variant of the Direction enum and must be handled explicitly in RTL logic; treating it as LTR in the component layer is the correct policy.
Proptest state machine tests live in `tests/proptest_state_machines/selection.rs` and require prop randomization for new behavioral axes (e.g., adding `dir` randomization for RTL fuzz coverage).

code.finding: tag_delete_trigger_attrs previously carried role=gridcell; it now lives on a new TagDeleteCell wrapper part so the button itself has no grid role, matching ARIA grid anatomy where gridcell wraps the interactive child.
SetValue must clamp both focused_index and edit_index to the new tag list length, and must exit editing mode if the currently-edited tag index no longer exists in the new value.
CommitEdit should only emit a ValueChange event when the tag list actually changes (i.e., the edited text differs from the original); rejected edits (same text) must not fire the event.
Inactive tag-edit inputs require hidden + aria-hidden + tabindex=-1 to be fully excluded from focus order and assistive technology; omitting any one of these leaves a gap.
RTL fuzz coverage requires adding dir (LTR/RTL/Auto) to the proptest arbitrary Props so the state machine exercises arrow-key reversal under randomized direction values.

workflow: Codex review runs in numbered passes; pass-2 findings are fetched as threads and triaged before implementation — clear bugs are applied directly, genuine design decisions are escalated to the user first.

When an ARIA design decision conflicts with the current spec anatomy, stopping to get user confirmation before implementing prevents rework on a structural change that touches enums, dispatch, conformance tests, and the spec simultaneously.
Clippy warnings should be resolved before running the coverage gate, since the gate includes a clippy step and failures late in the gate waste CI time.

Snapshot diffs should be reviewed manually before acceptance to verify only the intended attr changes appear; unexpected diffs in the snapshot output indicate a regression in a neighboring attr method.

friction: Direction::Auto was not handled in the initial RTL arrow-reversal implementation and required a follow-up edit after the first pass.
3 clippy warnings surfaced after the main implementation pass and had to be cleaned up before the coverage gate.
9 snapshots needed manual review and explicit acceptance after intentional attr changes — a necessary but interrupting step mid-implementation.
open_items: Codex pass-2 thread replies, resolution, and re-trigger were planned after the push but the transcript ends before confirming they were completed.

The `xci-fast` gate was run while capturing thread reply targets in parallel; if the gate had failed the reply step would have been premature — worth verifying gate-then-reply ordering is enforced in the workflow.
